EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moving eastward from the eastern Great Lakes, triggered strong thunderstorms in New York state, which moved into southern New England overnight.  The atmosphere was very unstable and surface dewpoints were near 70 degrees. The result was strong to locally severe thunderstorms before daybreak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Hampton, a tree and wires were down on Cherry Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level short wave trough moving through northern New England combined with an advancing cold front to produce severe thunderstorms in the late afternoon and evening hours.  Despite low K Indices and dry air aloft, the storms managed to strengthen and the dry air helped enhance the severe wind gusts.  The storms mainly impacted areas from western MA east-southeastward into northern CT and northern RI.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Windsor Locks, a house on Juniper Drive had a fire in the attack and in the walls, likely due to a lightning strike.  Report relayed via amateur radio operators.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Enfield at 614 PM EDT, police reported a tree was down on Mathewson Avenue...relayed by amateur radio.  Also in Enfield, at 616 PM EDT, amateur radio operators reported a large tree was down on Bailey Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front triggered isolated severe thunderstorms in eastern Connecticut during the early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Columbia, a billboard was blown down on Route 66 East.  Trees were downed on Cherry Valley Road, Flanders River Road, Cards Mill Road, and Old Willimantic Road.  A tree and wires were down on both Newberry Road and on Devonshire Lane, per the Fire Department.  Reports were relayed to NWS via amateur radio operators.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system centered near the eastern Great Lakes Region continued to strengthen as it moved northeast on December 23, 2022. This brought high winds over southern Connecticut that morning as the pressure gradient tightened over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple instances of downed trees with road closures occurred across Southern New London County as reported by the CT Department of highways. This included a tree with wires down in Gales Ferry, CT on Route 12 at Long Cove Road at 722 AM Local Standard Time and a tree with wires down in North Stonington, CT on Route 184 at Rocky Hollow Road at 813 AM Local Standard Time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system centered near the eastern Great Lakes Region continued to strengthen as it moved northeast on December 23, 2022. This brought high winds over southern Connecticut that morning as the pressure gradient tightened over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind gust of 61 mph was recorded at a mesonet station located in Madison, CT at 646 AM Local Standard Time at an elevation of 46 feet above ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system centered near the eastern Great Lakes Region continued to strengthen as it moved northeast on December 23, 2022. This brought high winds over southern Connecticut that morning as the pressure gradient tightened over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple instances of downed trees occurred in Northern New London County. The CT Department of Highways reported a downed tree and wires with a road closure on Route 201 at Sibicky Road in Griswold, CT at 635 AM Local Standard Time. Additionally, broadcast media provided a photo confirming trees down over Lisbon Heights Road in Lisbon, CT at 706 AM Local Standard Time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front triggered isolated severe thunderstorms in eastern Connecticut during the early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Windham, a tree and wires were down on South Windham Road.  Report relayed by amateur radio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Coastal low pressure along the polar front developed in response to an upper level disturbance. This result in an moderate snowfall across some western portions of Southern Connecticut, with heavier snow across more most eastern locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS Official observer at Bridgeport measured 8.4 inches of snow. Reports of generally 6 to 8.6 inches of snow were reported. These reports were from trained spotters and the public.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Coastal low pressure along the polar front developed in response to an upper level disturbance. This result in an moderate snowfall across some western portions of Southern Connecticut, with heavier snow across more most eastern locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A general 9 to 12 inches was reported from trained spotters and the public. A 9 inch public report came from Cheshire, while another public report of 12 inches came from Waterbury. Northern Madison came in with 10.9 inches from a trained spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Coastal low pressure along the polar front developed in response to an upper level disturbance. This result in an moderate snowfall across some western portions of Southern Connecticut, with heavier snow across more most eastern locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports of 4.5 to 7.6 inches of snow were reported. A trained spotter in Danbury reported 7.6 inches, and a 7.5 inch report was submitted via CoCorahs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Coastal low pressure along the polar front developed in response to an upper level disturbance. This result in an moderate snowfall across some western portions of Southern Connecticut, with heavier snow across more most eastern locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 7.5 to 10.5 inches were reported from the public, trained spotters, and broadcast media. A trained spotter reported 10.5 inches in Guilford, a trained spotter reported 9.0 inches in Milford, and a broadcast media reported 9.0 inches in New Haven, with another trained spotter reporting 9.5 inches in North Haven.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Coastal low pressure along the polar front developed in response to an upper level disturbance. This result in an moderate snowfall across some western portions of Southern Connecticut, with heavier snow across more most eastern locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 10.8 inches was reported in Middletown by broadcast media, and a report of 8.8 inches was reported via the public. Along the southern boundary of the zone a report of 11.0 inches in Higganum was reported via the public.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Coastal low pressure along the polar front developed in response to an upper level disturbance. This result in an moderate snowfall across some western portions of Southern Connecticut, with heavier snow across more most eastern locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Along the norther portion of the zone boundary a public report of 11 inches was reported in Higganum. A trained spotter reported 7.0 inches of snow in Westbrook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Nor'easter tracked just east of the benchmark bringing high snowfall totals and strong winds. The highest snowfall totals occurred primarily across eastern sections, with blizzard conditions verified  for New London County. Widespread wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph were reported across Connecticut, with the strongest gusts from New Haven on east where gusts up to and over 57 mph were reported. Blowing and drifting of snow resulted in drifts of over 3 feet across eastern sections especially. Widespread amounts of 12 to 18 inches fell across the eastern half of Southern Connecticut, with amounts mostly 12 inches or less across New Haven and Fairfield counties. The highest snowfall occurred across New London County with multiple reports of over 20 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous hours of 1/4 mile visibility with gusts over 35 mph with heavy snowfall as indicated by KGON ASOS, radar, and wind reports verified blizzard conditions across New London County. Peak wind gusts of 57 mph was reported at NDBC 2 S Groton, 57 mph at Stonington Outer Breakwater, and 65 mph at USGC Academy mesonet just north of New London. Numerous reports of snow drifts to 3 and 3 and a half feet. There were also numerous snowfall reports of 20 inches or more.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Coastal low pressure along the polar front developed in response to an upper level disturbance. This result in an moderate snowfall across some western portions of Southern Connecticut, with heavier snow across more most eastern locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public snowfall reports of 7.0 inches was measured in Franklin, and 7.0 inches in Colchester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Coastal low pressure along the polar front developed in response to an upper level disturbance. This result in an moderate snowfall across some western portions of Southern Connecticut, with heavier snow across more most eastern locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 5.3 inches of snow in Ledyard, and a public report came in with 5.1 inches from Ledyard Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Explosive cyclogenesis of a low pressure center off the Mid Atlantic coast brought a strong winter storm with blizzard conditions to all of southern New England Saturday and Saturday night. The storm tracked from east of the Carolinas to the 70w/40n benchmark bringing extreme snowfall rates of 2 to 4 inches per hour and winds gusting to hurricane force along the coast and 50 to 60 mph inland. The heaviest snow, just under 30 inches, fell over southeast MA and up to 2 feet fell in eastern CT. Snow tapered off overnight Saturday with quiet weather to follow on Sunday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall generally ranged from 3 to 13 inches. The highest amounts were in eastern Hartford county with lesser amounts to the west. The highest report was 13.8 inches in Manchester. Winds gusted 30 to 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southwest flow aloft overran a retreating and shallow cold air mass along a weak stationary boundary. This resulted in a brief but impactful period of wintry mix of precipitation which included freezing rain during the early morning hours and into the initial stages of the morning commute, but most impacts occurred just before the start of the main part of the morning commute.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing Rain resulted in an ice accretion of 0.04 inches at Meriden Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system approached from the southwest at the surface along with a closed upper level low. A broken like of convection produced strong to severe wind gusts across southern CT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bridgeport ASOS measured a 54 KT wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A intense low pressure system tracks just inland from the coast as high pressure retreated resulting in wintry precipitation changing quickly to rain along the coast. The changeover took longer for northwestern sections where accumulating snow fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A general 2 to 5 inches of snow fell across Northern portions of Fairfield County. Trained spotters reported 5.0 inches in New Fairfield, 3.0 inches in Shelton, and 1.5 inches in Weston. Cocorahs reported 2.0 inches in Ridgefield and 1.5 inches in Monroe. Danbury ASOS reported .05 inches of ice accretion after the snow changed to freezing rain.

Snow began around midnight and continued into Friday with the period of greatest snow accumulation in the early to late morning hours. This impacted travel during the morning commute. 10 to 14 inches fell in the I-95 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall ranged from 6 to 13 inches with the highest amount (13.2 inches) reported in Columbia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary off the East Coast resulted in a period of light freezing rain across portions of northwestern Connecticut during the morning hours on Wednesday, January 5. While amounts were only around a light glaze, icy conditions resulted in numerous vehicle accidents.||Key Impacts: travel delays, vehicle accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two drivers were sent to the hospital with injuries after a vehicle accident on Route 44 near Lower Road in the Town of North Canaan. This accident resulted in a closure of Route 44 for a time due to a downed utility pole and some wires.

The Bridgeport ASOS reported at 47 mph gust, and the Norwalk, Greenwich, and Fairfield CWOP stations reported gusts of 49, 48, and 42 mph respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure pushed across the region resulting in primarily freezing rain across northwestern Connecticut. Freezing rain began in the early morning hours and continued into the afternoon hours before tapering off and temperatures rising above freezing. Some sleet and snowflakes mixed in across some areas but was rather brief. Overall flat ice accumulations of around 0.10 of an inch occurred. No significant impacts occurred from this event.||Key impacts: none.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst occurred in Willimantic.  The 911 Call Center in Willimantic reported that a chimney fell into a roof on West Main Street at 608 PM EDT (508 PM EST).  Police reported that the top of a utility pole was snapped off at the intersection of Pleasant Street and Lebanon Avenue.  A tree and wires were down on Pleasant Street.  A tree and wires also were down on West Avenue, South Street at Oxbow Drive, and Southridge Drive.  Reports were relayed to NWS via amateur radio operators.

The pressure gradient tightened as a cold front approached from the west. This produced strong winds over Southern Connecticut early in the morning of February 18, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple high wind gusts occurred over Southern London County. A gust of 60 mph was recorded at Groton Airport at 540 AM Local Standard Time. At nearby New London Ledge Light (2 miles south of Groton), a gust of 62 mph was recorded at 66 ft above ground at 556 AM Local Standard Time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved into the Berkshires late in the afternoon, out ahead of a cold front.  The storms weakened as they moved east but still caused isolated tree/wire damage in the slopes of the Berkshires and in Hartford County, CT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Hartford, a tree was downed, along with wires, on Holcomb Street.  Wires also were downed on Barbour Street.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports of 5.5 inches for Jewett City via broadcast media, and 4.5 inches in the Franklin area via the public were provided.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front which behaved like an anafront as the the upper level flow was parallel to the front brought advisory level freezing rain to portions of the area. The highest |freezing rain reports were under 0.15 inches (flat).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Danbury Airport ASOS reported 0.14 inches of flat ice accretion. Other ice reports further south outside of Northern Fairfield were far less and on the order of only 0.02 or less of ice accretion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure pushed across northwestern Connecticut on Saturday, February 19, 2022. A passing warm front brought a period of snow to the region during the late morning and afternoon hours. Snow amounts ranged from a coating to two inches.||In the wake of this snow, snow showers and squalls developed ahead of a cold front during the afternoon hours. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved northeast along the Tennessee Valley and the Lower Appalachains and redeveloped along the Mid Atlantic coast as it moved northeast. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged winter storm began on Thursday, February 3 and continued into Friday, February 4. Precipitation began as rain on February 3. Colder air gradually pushed southward into the overnight and early morning hours of February 4 resulting in a changeover to frozen precipitation, primarily freezing rain, with just a brief period of sleet and snow at the end. Flat ice accumulations  of 0.10 to 0.20 of an inch were observed with up to a coating of snow and sleet. Precipitation ended during the afternoon hours on February 4.||Key impacts: travel delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of hot and humid weather occurred across northwestern Connecticut during the second half of July. Heat indices peaked between 95 and 103 degrees during the afternoon hours on July 20, 21, 23 and 24. Governor Ned Lamont activated Connecticut's extreme hot weather protocol from July 19 to July 24 which allowed state agencies, municipalities and others to coordinate with United Way 2-1-1 to ensure information on cooling centers was available statewide.

A thunderstorm developed across northwestern Connecticut during the early evening hours on July 28, 2022 producing a weak EF-0 tornado near Colebrook, which downed some trees and damaged some structures along its path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-0 tornado touched down about 2 miles south-southwest of Dennis Hill State Park along Smith Road and traveled northeastward for about 5 miles before ending about 2 miles southwest of Colebrook along Pinney Street. Damage was sporadic and consisted mostly of downed hardwood trees and broken tree branches. A couple of barns along Pinney Street lost some siding and one of the barns had a door torn off. There were many eye witnesses which provided both video and pictures of the tornado.

As low pressure associated with the remnants of Nicole moved along the Appalachians and into western New England, a strong southerly low level jet developed late at night on the 11th across southern New England.  Strong wind gusts occurred overnight in northern Connecticut, Rhode Island, and eastern Massachusetts.  In southeast Massachusetts, a fine line of convection moved through in the early morning of the 12th, which also enhanced wind gusts.  The highest wind gusts reported were in the 40-47 mph range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Windham, at 824 PM EST, a 1 foot diameter tree was down on Plains Road.  Also, at 1043 PM EST, a tree fell onto a house on Oakwood Drive, per Fire Dept.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As low pressure associated with the remnants of Nicole moved along the Appalachians and into western New England, a strong southerly low level jet developed late at night on the 11th across southern New England.  Strong wind gusts occurred overnight in northern Connecticut, Rhode Island, and eastern Massachusetts.  In southeast Massachusetts, a fine line of convection moved through in the early morning of the 12th, which also enhanced wind gusts.  The highest wind gusts reported were in the 40-47 mph range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Glastonbury at 445 AM EST, a tree was down on wires on Newell Lane.  Confirmed via photo on Twitter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The United States Drought Monitor (USDM) continued to indicate severe drought conditions across much of Litchfield County in Connecticut through the month of September.||Precipitation the previous 30 days were about 50 to 75 percent of normal but below normal precipitation signals had persisted since the start of June. Above normal temperatures combining with below normal precipitation contributed to the rapid deterioration of drought conditions. Soaking rainfall events during the month of September gradually improved drought conditions in some areas.||The Connecticut Interagency Drought Workgroup continued the issuance of a Stage 2 Drought Declaration for Litchfield County. Residents and businesses were asked to voluntarily reduce water usage to minimize the strain on the water supply.

A strong cold front moved across southern New England, causing southerly winds that gusted to 40 to 55 mph.  Although leaves were gone from many trees, there was still some scattered tree damage in northern Connecticut, northern Rhode Island, and in  Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In New Britain, a tree was down on Adams Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The pressure gradient tightened as a cold front approached from the west. This produced high winds over Southern Connecticut in the late afternoon to early evening hours of November 30, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind gust of 63 mph was recorded at a mesonet station located in Hammonasset, CT at 446 PM Local Standard Time. Additionally, broadcast media reported trees and wires down on Race Hill Road in Guilford, CT at 534 PM Local Standard Time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The pressure gradient tightened as a cold front approached from the west. This produced high winds over Southern Connecticut in the late afternoon to early evening hours of November 30, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple high wind gusts were recorded at mesonet sites, including 59 mph at 312 PM Local Standard Time 2 miles ESE of Darien and 58 mph at 220 PM Local Standard Time 2 miles W of Stamford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front draped over the area on September 5th and 6th allowed for the development of slow moving thunderstorms. These thunderstorms produced torrential rainfall in an environment with precipitable water values 1.8-2 inches. Anywhere from 2 to 4 inches of rainfall fell in a several hour period with some localized spots receiving close to 6 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 138 is closed in both directions between Route 169 and Ross Road due to flooding in the Town of Lisbon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The United States Drought Monitor (USDM) indicated a small area of severe drought across Litchfield County in Connecticut starting on August 9, 2022. The previous 30 days had seen less than half of normal rainfall with below normal precipitation persisting since the start of June. A period of hot and dry weather, especially during the first several days of August, contributed to this rapid deterioration of drought conditions. The severe drought expanded further to include more of Litchfield County starting on August 16. Severe drought in these areas continued into the month of September.||The Connecticut Interagency Drought Workgroup continued the issuance of a Stage 2 Drought Declaration for Litchfield County. Residents and businesses were asked to voluntarily reduce water usage to minimize the strain on the water supply.
A dome of high pressure brought a stretch of hot and humid weather resulting in multiple days of heat indices between 95F and 104F degrees across northwestern Connecticut between August 4-9, 2022.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough of low pressure in western New England combined with instability and just enough shear to produce a broken line of severe thunderstorms in western MA and northern CT in the mid afternoon. A significant microburst occurred in Erving and Warwick, MA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Southington, a tree was reported down on three roads... Summit Street, Marion Avenue, and Long Bottom Road.  Reports relayed by amateur radio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough of low pressure in western New England combined with instability and just enough shear to produce a broken line of severe thunderstorms in western MA and northern CT in the mid afternoon. A significant microburst occurred in Erving and Warwick, MA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Wethersfield, multiple wires were down on Olney Road; large branches were down in a backyard on Clearfield Road; wires were down on Harold Street; multiple wires and a branch were down on Olney Road; a branch was down on wires on Church Street; and a branch was on top of the roof of a house on Prospect Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system brought widespread accumulating snowfall across northwestern Connecticut on December 11, 2022 before tapering off during the early morning hours on December 12, 2022. Many areas picked up between 3 and 6 inches of snow.||Key impacts: snow covered roads, travel delays.

A cold front stalled over southern New England causing very heavy rain and significant flash flooding before the front moved off the south coast during the evening. Rainfall amounts ranged from 7 to 11 inches in eastern Connecticut and the Providence metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Sterling, a trained spotter reported a 24 hour total of 7.20 inches of rain. A CoCoRaHS observer reported 6.91 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two separate frontal systems merged to form a powerful winter storm over the Great Lakes from December 22 to December 24 bringing multiple hazards to the region including heavy rain, strong winds and a very cold air mass in its wake. Rainfall amounts of 1 to 2.50 inches were common across the county. No flooding was reported from this event across Litchfield County.||The main impact across Litchfield County was from high winds leading to downed trees and power outages. More than 17,000 customers were without power at some point during the storm.||The arrival of a cold air mass led to wind chill values falling to between 15 and 20 degrees below zero across northern portions of Litchfield County.||Key impacts: tree damage, power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down throughout the county with numerous power outages.
